params.text:
Trust your instincts and act

Your safety comes before any task. If a situation feels wrong, you do not need to wait for something to happen before acting. It is always acceptable to leave. No visit is worth being harmed.

Leave, alarm, report

  • Leave: make an exit calmly and without confrontation. You do not have to explain or apologise.
  • Raise the alarm: use your lone-worker device, phone or agreed signal to alert your team, and call 999 if you are in immediate danger.
  • Report: once safe, tell your manager exactly what happened so the risk assessment can be updated and colleagues protected.

Reporting near misses matters just as much as reporting incidents. A concern you raise today may prevent harm to the next worker who visits.

params.alt: Illustration for If you feel unsafe

params.question: If a visit feels unsafe, you should stay and finish the task before telling anyone, so the care is not missed.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. No task is worth your safety. If a visit feels unsafe you should leave, raise the alarm, and report it afterwards.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Your safety comes first. It is always acceptable to leave, raise the alarm and then report what happened so it can be dealt with.

params.text:
Key points to take away

  • You are a lone worker whenever you work without a colleague on hand, including on visits and night shifts.
  • No law bans lone working, but the Health and Safety at Work Act requires the risks to be assessed and controlled.
  • Follow your safe systems every time: check in and out, carry a charged phone, share your schedule and use your alarm.
  • Keep asking "does this feel safe?" and act on the answer.
  • If you feel unsafe: leave, raise the alarm, and report.
